content:
# Deuteronomy Chapter 21 — SOPHIA Consensus Translation

**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **Sources:** EMET (literal), LOGOS (formal), PNEUMA (dynamic) | **Chapter:** Deuteronomy 21 (23 verses) | **LC:** pp. 218–219 | **Vaticanus:** p. 156

---

**1** If a slain person is found in the land that the LORD your God is giving you to possess, lying in the open field, and it is not known who struck him down, **2** then your elders and your judges shall go out and measure the distance to the cities that are around the slain person. **3** And the city that is nearest to the slain man—the elders of that city shall take a heifer of the herd that has not been worked, one that has not pulled in a yoke. **4** And the elders of that city shall bring the heifer down to a valley with running water, where no work has been done and nothing has been sown, and shall break the heifer's neck there in the valley. **5** Then the priests, the sons of Levi, shall come forward, for the LORD your God has chosen them to minister to him and to pronounce blessings in the name of the LORD, and by their word every dispute and every assault shall be settled. **6** And all the elders of that city nearest to the slain man shall wash their hands over the heifer whose neck was broken in the valley, **7** and they shall declare and say: "Our hands did not shed this blood, and our eyes did not see it. **8** Accept atonement, O LORD, for your people Israel, whom you have redeemed, and do not set the guilt of innocent blood in the midst of your people Israel." And the blood-guilt shall be atoned for them. **9** So you shall purge the guilt of innocent blood from your midst, when you do what is right in the eyes of the LORD.

**10** When you go out to war against your enemies, and the LORD your God gives them into your hand and you take them captive, **11** and you see among the captives a woman of beautiful form, and you desire her and want to take her as your wife— **12** then you shall bring her into your house, and she shall shave her head and pare her nails. **13** And she shall take off the garments of her captivity and shall remain in your house and weep for her father and her mother a full month. After that you may go in to her and be her husband, and she shall be your wife. **14** But if you find no delight in her, you shall let her go wherever she wishes; you shall not sell her for silver, nor shall you treat her as a slave, because you have humiliated her.

**15** If a man has two wives, the one loved and the other unloved, and both have borne him sons, and if the firstborn son belongs to the unloved— **16** then on the day he assigns his possessions as an inheritance to his sons, he may not treat the son of the loved as the firstborn in preference to the son of the unloved, who is the actual firstborn. **17** But he shall acknowledge the firstborn, the son of the unloved, by giving him a double portion of all that he has, for he is the first of his vigor. The right of the firstborn is his.

**18** If a man has a stubborn and rebellious son who will not obey the voice of his father or the voice of his mother, and though they discipline him he will not listen to them— **19** then his father and his mother shall take hold of him and bring him out to the elders of his city at the gate of his city. **20** And they shall say to the elders of his city: "This son of ours is stubborn and rebellious; he will not obey our voice—he is a glutton and a drunkard." **21** Then all the men of his city shall stone him to death with stones. So you shall purge the evil from your midst, and all Israel shall hear and fear.

**22** And if a man has committed a sin worthy of death and he is put to death, and you hang him on a tree— **23** his body shall not remain overnight on the tree, but you shall bury him on that day, for a hanged man is a curse of God. You shall not defile your land that the LORD your God is giving you as an inheritance.

---

## SOPHIA's Council Notes

**Chapter overview.** Deuteronomy 21 is a chapter of miscellaneous laws: the broken-necked heifer rite for an unsolved murder (*eglah arufah*, vv. 1–9), the law of the captive woman (vv. 10–14), the rights of the firstborn (vv. 15–17), the stubborn and rebellious son (vv. 18–21), and burial of the executed (vv. 22–23). The three agents show their largest divergence in vv. 4, 8, 14, and 23.

**v.4 "valley with running water" (LOGOS) vs. "wadi with running water" (EMET) vs. "valley where there is a stream that never runs dry" (PNEUMA).** Hebrew *nachal eitan* = "perennial/ever-flowing wadi." EMET's "wadi" is technically more precise (*nachal* = seasonal stream-valley); LOGOS's "valley" is more accessible. PNEUMA adds "that never runs dry" (expanding *eitan*). Consensus follows LOGOS ("valley with running water") as the most accessible rendering that preserves the flowing-water element. EMET's note that *nachal* technically means "wadi" is acknowledged.

**v.5 "sons of Levi" (EMET/LOGOS) vs. "Levitical priests" (PNEUMA).** EMET/LOGOS: "priests, the sons of Levi." PNEUMA: "Levitical priests." Both render the same group. Consensus follows EMET/LOGOS (more literal, retaining "sons of Levi" as the Deuteronomic designation).

**v.8 "Atone" (EMET) vs. "Accept atonement" (LOGOS) vs. "Accept this atonement" (PNEUMA).** Hebrew *kapper* (Piel imperative) = "atone, make atonement." EMET's "Atone, O LORD" is the bare imperative. LOGOS: "Accept atonement" makes God the recipient. PNEUMA adds "this." Consensus follows LOGOS ("Accept atonement") — slightly more natural as an address while preserving the imperative force.

**v.12 "pare her nails" vs. "do her nails" vs. "trim her nails."** All three render *aseta et-tsiporneyha* differently. EMET: "do her nails" (literal). LOGOS: "pare her nails." PNEUMA: "trim her nails." "Pare" (LOGOS) best conveys paring/cutting down without over-specifying the practice. Consensus follows LOGOS.

**v.14 "humiliated" (LOGOS) vs. "humbled" (EMET) vs. "made use of" (PNEUMA).** Hebrew *ineytah* (Pi'el of *anah*) = to humble, afflict, coerce. In this sexual context it implies violation/coercion. EMET: "humbled." LOGOS: "humiliated." PNEUMA: "made use of" (too euphemistic). Consensus follows LOGOS ("humiliated") as it carries the appropriate weight of acknowledged coercion.

**v.17 "first of his vigor" (EMET) vs. "firstfruits of his strength" (LOGOS) vs. "first evidence of his father's strength" (PNEUMA).** Hebrew *reshit ono* = "beginning of his vigor/strength." EMET: "first of his vigor." LOGOS: "firstfruits of his strength." PNEUMA: "first evidence of his father's strength" (adds "evidence" and "father's"). Consensus follows EMET ("first of his vigor") — most compact and literal while preserving the biological-force metaphor.

**vv. 22–23 "a curse of God" vs. "under God's curse."** All three render *qilelat Elohim* differently in emphasis. EMET: "a curse of God" (literal genitive — ambiguous, preserved). LOGOS: "a curse of God." PNEUMA: "under God's curse." Consensus follows EMET/LOGOS: "a curse of God" preserves the Hebrew genitive ambiguity that generated significant theological interpretation (including Paul's use in Gal 3:13). Vaticanus reads *kekataramenos hypo Theou* ("cursed by God"), resolving the ambiguity; consensus follows MT.

---

*Arbiter: SOPHIA*
